title: Benchmarking Complex Instruction-Following with Multiple Constraints Composition
authors: Bosi Wen, Pei Ke, Xiaotao Gu, Lindong Wu, Hao Huang, Jinfeng Zhou, Wenchuang Li, Binxin Hu, Wendy Gao, Jiaxin Xu, Yiming Liu, Jie Tang, Hongning Wang, Minlie Huang (Tsinghua University, Zhipu AI, UESTC, China University of Geosciences, Central China Normal University; Bosi Wen & Pei Ke equal contribution; Minlie Huang corresponding)
venue: NeurIPS 2024 Datasets and Benchmarks Track (arXiv 2407.03978; v3, 31 Oct 2024)
claim: Existing complex-instruction-following benchmarks model constraints but neglect how constraints are structurally *composed* (And, Chain, Selection, Nested), which biases evaluation and undercounts real difficulty. The paper introduces ComplexBench — a hierarchical taxonomy (4 constraint types, 19 dimensions, 4 composition types) plus a manually-annotated 1,150-instruction dataset and a Rule-Augmented LLM-based (RAL) evaluator with dependency-aware score aggregation (DRFR) — and shows that all 15 tested LLMs degrade sharply as composition complexity (especially Chain and Selection, and their nesting) increases, with even GPT-4-1106 failing ~20% of instructions overall and only 14.9% coherent-test accuracy on multi-layer nested Selection.
novelty: Authors present the contribution as: (1) the first taxonomy/dataset to explicitly model composition types beyond simple And-coordination — introducing Chain (sequential sub-tasks) and Selection (conditional branch selection) and their Nested combinations, motivated by a real-world usage-log analysis showing professional instructions contain more Selection/nested structure than prior benchmarks cover (Sec 3.3, Fig 4); (2) a new evaluation method (Rule-Augmented LLM-based evaluation, RAL) that combines an LLM extractor + deterministic rule-checker for rule-verifiable scoring questions with a pure LLM judge for open-ended ones, and aggregates via a composition-derived dependency structure (Sec 4.2, Eq. 1-4) rather than naive averaging, which they show improves agreement with human judgments over both plain LLM-judging and Direct Scoring (Tables 3-4).

protocol.description: Primary evaluation (Table 5, Sec 5.2.1) is single-shot: each of 15 LLMs is given the full complex instruction I (which may itself encode a Chain of sequential sub-tasks or a Selection with a condition) in one prompt/one generation call (greedy decoding, max length 8,192), producing one response o. Evaluation of that single response is then done off-model by a separate GPT-4-1106 judge pipeline (RAL, Sec 4.2): for each scoring question q attached to the instruction (per constraint/composition), an LLM extractor pulls the relevant span e = M_ext(I,q,o), then either a deterministic rule R_q(e) or an LLM judge M_eva(I,q,o) produces r_q in {0,1}; results are combined via dependency aggregation r_q' = r_q AND (AND over p in Dep(q) of r_p) and finally aggregated into DRFR = sum(r_ij')/sum(m_i) across all instructions/questions. A second, separate experiment (Sec 5.2.3 'Decomposition') is explicitly interactive/multi-turn: composition-bearing instructions are manually decomposed into their constituent sub-tasks (Chain -> sequential tasks; Selection -> selection + execution branches; And left intact) and the model executes them step-by-step across multiple rounds, with scoring questions likewise split and compared 1:1 against the original single-shot scores (Table 6) to isolate the effect of interaction structure from task content.
protocol.observation_content: Single-shot generation call: the model sees only the full natural-language instruction I as given (e.g., 'Write a blog post ... with the title indicated by double angle brackets ... ensure the number of sentences is less than 5 ... reach 250 [words]' — Table 12 example), with no scoring questions, no constraint labels, and no in-context demonstration of the constraint taxonomy — the model must infer and satisfy all embedded constraints/compositions from the prose alone. The evaluator (not the generation model) separately sees a structured prompt per scoring question: the extractor prompt (Table 9) shows '[Input Instruction] {...} [Model Response] {...} [Scoring Question] {...}' plus 6 in-context examples and outputs an [Explanation] then a copied 'Scoring Object'; the evaluator prompt (Table 11) shows the same three fields plus detailed judgment rules and outputs 'Analysis: xxx / Answer: Yes/No'. In the decomposition experiment (Sec 5.2.3), the model instead receives a sequence of decomposed sub-instructions turn-by-turn and produces a response per turn, which is scored against the corresponding split scoring questions.

representations.variants_compared: The paper does not vary input representation/format of the instruction itself (e.g., no JSON-vs-prose ablation on the generation side). The representational variants that are compared are on the *evaluation* side: (1) RAL vs 'RAL w/o rule' (LLM-only, no deterministic rule check) vs 'Direct Scoring' (a 1-10 holistic quality score prompt) for judging responses (Tables 3-4); (2) presenting all scoring questions to the judge simultaneously vs one at a time, and forcing a bare YES/NO vs requiring an analysis-then-answer format — 'we found that the current settings [one question at a time, with analysis] achieve the highest level of agreement with humans' (Appendix E.2, prose after Table 11).
representations.effect_on_accuracy: RAL achieves 0.614 Overall Pairwise Agreement with humans vs 0.574 (RAL w/o Dependency Aggregation) vs 0.512 (Direct Scoring) (Table 3). At question level, RAL reaches 95.36% agreement on rule-defined questions vs 82.02% (RAL w/o rule) vs 62.02% (Direct Scoring), and 87.82% overall vs 85.56% (w/o rule) vs 75.18% (Direct Scoring) (Table 4) — i.e., removing the deterministic rule component costs the most agreement specifically on rule-verifiable constraint types (13+ points), while open-ended question agreement is unaffected by the rule component (86.28% either way, Table 4).

models.name: GPT-4-1106
strategies: also used as the judge/evaluator model (GPT-4-1106) for RAL scoring of all models' outputs
models.key_results: Highest All(Avg.) DRFR = 0.800 (Table 5), but still fails ~20% of complex instructions ('the widely recognized powerful GPT-4 still fails to complete 20% of complex instructions' — Sec 5.2.2). Selection(>=3) = 0.694, Sel.&Chain(>=3) = 0.626 (Table 5) — its worst subcategories. Achieves only 14.9% accuracy in the Coherent Test on multi-layer nested Selection instructions (Sec 5.2.3).

models.name: Claude-3-Opus
models.key_results: All(Avg.) DRFR = 0.788 (Table 5); And(1)=0.886 (highest of all models on And); Selection(>=3)=0.592 (its weakest subcategory).

models.name: GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106
strategies: decomposed/multi-round instruction-following (manual decomposition into Chain sub-tasks / Selection branches, executed step-by-step)
models.key_results: All(Avg.) DRFR = 0.682 single-shot (Table 5). Under manual decomposition into multi-round execution, overall DRFR drops to 0.652 (Delta -0.030), with the largest drops on the most composed categories: Selection(>=3) 0.475->0.411 (Delta -0.064), Sel.&Chain(>=3) 0.482->0.415 (Delta -0.067), while And(1) is unchanged (0.845->0.845, Delta 0.000) (Table 6). Fine-grained composition-count breakdown (Table 21, Appendix I.1): DRFR falls from 0.845 (1 And only) to 0.083 for the single hardest combination (1 And + 1 Chain + 2 Selection).

models.name: Qwen1.5-72B-Chat
models.key_results: All(Avg.) DRFR = 0.752 (Table 5), below GPT-4 on ComplexBench (Table 7); on the IFEval/HumanEval/MATH comparison table (Table 7) Qwen1.5-72B-Chat scores 55.8/71.3/42.5, notably lower than Llama-3-70B-Instruct's 78.9/81.7/50.4, so Qwen1.5-72B-Chat is NOT the highest open-source model on those three benchmarks (that distinction belongs to Llama-3-70B-Instruct).

models.name: Llama-3-70B-Instruct
models.key_results: All(Avg.) DRFR = 0.757 (Table 5), underlined as best open-source overall. Surpasses GPT-4-1106 on IFEval (78.9 vs 75.4) yet trails it on ComplexBench (0.757 vs 0.800) — cited as evidence ComplexBench captures complexity/composition ability that IFEval does not (Sec 5.2.3 'Comparisons between Other Capabilities', Table 7).

models.name: InternLM2-20B-Chat / Qwen1.5-14B-Chat / Baichuan2-13B-Chat / Llama-3-8B-Instruct / Mistral-7B-Instruct / Qwen1.5-7B-Chat / InternLM2-7B-Chat / ChatGLM3-6B-Chat
models.key_results: All(Avg.) DRFR range from 0.546 (ChatGLM3-6B-Chat, lowest of all 15 models) to 0.680 (Qwen1.5-14B-Chat) among this group (Table 5; InternLM2-20B-Chat = 0.678, a close second). 'the performance of most open-source LLMs falls short compared to closed-source LLMs especially on complex composition types' (Sec 5.2.2).

headline_numbers: GPT-4-1106 fails ~20% of complex instructions overall, All(Avg.) DRFR = 0.800, the best of 15 models (Table 5; Sec 5.2.2).
Performance drops sharply and monotonically with composition complexity across all models, worst on Selection and Chain: e.g. GPT-4-1106 And(1)=0.881 -> Sel.&Chain(>=3)=0.626 (Table 5).
Fine-grained nesting analysis: GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106 DRFR collapses from 0.845 (single And) to 0.083 (1 And + 1 Chain + 2 Selection combined) (Table 21, Appendix I.1).

Manual decomposition into multi-round/step-by-step execution does NOT rescue performance: GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106 overall DRFR drops 0.682 -> 0.652 (Delta -0.030) when decomposed, with larger drops (up to -0.067) on more composed categories, i.e. multi-turn execution accumulates errors rather than helping (Table 6, Sec 5.2.3).

Coherent Test on Selection: even GPT-4 achieves only 14.9% accuracy across all correct-branch positions on multi-layer nested Selection instructions; smaller models 'can't perfectly follow any group of instructions' (Sec 5.2.3, Fig 7).

RAL evaluation method agreement with humans: 0.614 pairwise (vs 0.512 Direct Scoring) (Table 3); 87.82% question-level overall agreement (vs 75.18% Direct Scoring), with rule-defined-subset agreement at 95.36% RAL vs 62.02% Direct Scoring (Table 4).

Across constraint dimensions (Fig 6 / Appendix I.2 prose), Length, Punctuation, Keywords, End-with, and Factuality are the hardest dimensions; even the strongest model reaches only 0.532 accuracy on Length (Appendix I.2).
failure_modes: All models show significant, monotonic accuracy decline as composition complexity (nesting depth, number of Chain/Selection branches) increases (Sec 5.2.2, Table 5, Table 21).

Selection composition is uniquely hard: the paper speculates 'the main difficulty in Selection lies not only in choosing the correct branch but in executing it without interference from irrelevant branches' (Sec 5.2.2) — i.e. models leak content/constraints from non-selected branches.

Nested/multi-layer Selection is the single worst structural pattern: 'Nested multiple Selection seems to be significantly more difficult than other composition type nested methods' (Appendix I.1), and coherent-test accuracy (requiring correctness across all branch positions of a condition) falls to 14.9% even for GPT-4 (Sec 5.2.3).

Manual instruction decomposition into a multi-turn execution loop makes things worse, not better, attributed to 'cumulative errors in multi-round interactions' (Sec 5.2.3, Table 6) — composition cannot be trivially routed around via multi-step prompting.

Format and Lexical constraint types (which have explicit, objective evaluation standards — length, keywords, end-with, punctuation) are systematically harder for LLMs than Semantic/Utility constraints (Sec 5.2.2, Fig 6, Appendix I.2), suggesting a specific weakness in precise output planning/counting rather than general language quality.

Selection branch position bias: annotators show ~70% tendency to place the correct branch first in two-branch Selections (Sec 4.1 footnote), which the paper corrects for via Selection Branch Expansion — implying naive Selection instructions could inflate apparent model competence via guessing.

LLM-based evaluation alone (without rule augmentation) shows 'a significant deficiency' on rule-defined, objective/numerical scoring questions such as keyword inclusion and text length, while conversely rule-based methods alone are described as 'powerless against open-ended scoring problems' (Sec 4.2) — both weaknesses jointly motivate the hybrid RAL design. (Note: the paper's word 'powerless' applies to rule-based methods on open-ended questions, not to LLM-based methods on rule-defined questions.)
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's single-shot condition is the primary/default evaluation: models receive the full composed instruction and produce one response in one generation call (Sec 5.2.1, Table 5). The paper additionally and directly tests whether decomposing composition structure into an explicit multi-round interaction loop helps (Sec 5.2.3, 'Decomposition of instructions with composition types'): instructions are manually split by composition type (Chain -> sequential turns, Selection -> selection-then-execution turns, And left intact) and executed step-by-step, with scores compared 1:1 against the single-shot condition using matched/split scoring questions for fairness. The finding is that multi-round decomposition does not help and generally hurts performance (GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106 overall DRFR 0.682 -> 0.652, worse on more complex composition categories, Table 6), which the authors attribute to cumulative errors compounding across turns, and they conclude 'our benchmark is challenging and cannot be simply solved via instruction decomposition' (Sec 5.2.3). This is the paper's only multi-turn/interactive protocol; it is explicitly a diagnostic ablation, not proposed as a better solution strategy — the paper does not test few-shot/many-shot/CoT/algorithm-in-prompt strategies for the generation models at all (those axes are unverified — missing from extraction if present elsewhere, but no such ablation appears in the verbatim body/appendix text captured here).

relation_to_candidate.position: ComplexBench's primary protocol is single-shot generation (one full composed instruction to model, one response) evaluated by a separate off-model judge pipeline, with a secondary multi-turn ablation. What is observed by the generation model is the entire natural-language instruction only — no scoring rubric, no constraint labels, no worked demonstration of the taxonomy; constraints and their composition (And/Chain/Selection/Nested) must be inferred from prose. Output shape is a free-form full text response (a blog post, essay, dialogue, etc. — often hundreds of words per Table 2's #Len. column, average 477.5 characters per instruction and multi-sentence/multi-paragraph outputs), not a single fact/label. Scoring is NOT purely rule-based: it is a hybrid Rule-Augmented LLM-based (RAL) verifier — an LLM extractor pulls the relevant span, then either a deterministic rule (for rule-verifiable questions, ~17% of the meta-eval subset per Table 4) or an LLM judge (for open-ended questions) produces the 0/1 verdict, and per-question verdicts are combined via a composition-derived AND-dependency structure into a continuous DRFR score (partial credit across questions), not a strict all-or-nothing single scalar per instance.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c22: Direct conceptual overlap on exactly one axis: the Selection composition operator. ComplexBench formally defines Selection as choosing among branches {B1..Bm} via a selection function S(cond) that depends on a condition in the input (Sec 3.3), and shows this is empirically the hardest and most bias-prone composition type (branch-position bias corrected via Selection Branch Expansion, Sec 4.1; steep accuracy drop in Table 5/21; 14.9% GPT-4 coherent-test accuracy on nested Selection, Sec 5.2.3) — this is the structural ancestor of c22's conditional Selection constraints ('if input has property P obey rule A else rule B... must be discovered rather than copied'). Both also share the general idea of stacking/composing multiple independently-defined constraints and requiring all to hold.
relation_to_candidate.differences: Everything else differs substantially: (1) task scale — ComplexBench instructions are long free-form generation tasks (avg 477 chars in, multi-sentence/paragraph out) built around real writing/QA/role-play tasks, vs c22's deliberately trivial micro-task (a few words output); (2) constraint count/type — ComplexBench averages 4.19 constraints per instruction across 19 rich, often subjective dimensions (Topic, Sentiment, Language Style, Personalization, Helpfulness, Factuality, etc.), most NOT rule-verifiable (83% of scoring questions are open-ended per Table 4), vs c22's 3-5 constraints drawn entirely from a deterministic checker library (word count, forbidden letter, keyword, casing, end-with-token); (3) scoring — ComplexBench uses a hybrid LLM+rule judge with partial-credit DRFR aggregation and composition-dependency short-circuiting, explicitly NOT strict all-pass 0/1, vs c22's pure-rule, no-partial-credit, all-or-nothing 0/1 scoring with per-checker diagnostic logging; (4) Selection semantics — ComplexBench's Selection condition and branches are both stated in natural language within the instruction (the model must correctly parse and act on an explicit condition, e.g. 'Painting: Mona Lisa' vs 'Painting: Galloping horse', Sec 5.2.3), whereas c22 explicitly requires the property/condition to be DISCOVERED (not stated) by the model, which is a harder/different anti-enumeration mechanism than anything ComplexBench's Selection tests; (5) protocol — ComplexBench's main result is single-shot with an LLM judge, plus a secondary decomposition-into-multi-turn ablation that the authors show is neutral-to-harmful, whereas c22 is designed purely as single-call, oracle-scored.
relation_to_candidate.candidate_adds_beyond_paper: c22 would add: a fully deterministic, code-only verifier (no LLM judge anywhere in scoring) achieving exact reproducible 0/1 grading, which ComplexBench explicitly does not attempt for ~83% of its scoring questions (it relies on GPT-4-1106 as judge, Sec 5.2.1) and flags as a limitation (potential LLM-as-judge bias, cost, data leakage — Appendix A); a synthetic infinite/contamination-proof instance generator via a COLLIE-style grammar (ComplexBench is a fixed, manually-annotated 1,150-instance static dataset, Sec 4.1, with no generator released for new instances); an explicit discover-not-copy conditional mechanism, tightening ComplexBench's Selection (which states its condition in-instruction) into a genuinely hidden-property test; and a strict all-pass (no partial credit, no dependency-based partial aggregation) scoring rule intended to compress the pass/fail signal harder than ComplexBench's DRFR, trading graded diagnostic richness for a cleaner binary reward signal suited to RLVR-style optimization.

corpus_claim: ComplexBench (Wen et al., NeurIPS 2024 D&B) contributes the And/Chain/Selection/Nested composition operators; c22's conditional Selection constraints ('discover, not copy') come from here.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The paper does verifiably introduce and formally define exactly these four composition types — And, Chain, Selection, and Nested combinations of them (Sec 3.3, Fig 3; Table 1 explicitly lists 'And / Chain / Selection / Nested.' as ComplexBench's distinguishing columns vs prior benchmarks) — so the operator-taxonomy attribution is fully supported. However, the second half of the claim ('discover, not copy') is NOT literally supported by the paper: in every worked ComplexBench example and in the formal definition (Sec 3.3, 'Selection contains m branches ... taking the selection condition cond as input'), the selection condition is always stated explicitly within the instruction text (e.g., Table 12's Mona Lisa vs. Galloping-horse example in Sec 5.2.3) — the model is never required to infer or discover an unstated/hidden property of the input to determine which branch applies. c22's 'must be discovered rather than copied' framing is therefore a novel extension/hardening that this paper does not itself test or claim; the corpus citation is correct about the operator's origin but should not imply the paper already tests the hidden-condition variant.
baseline_relevant: Instance/difficulty dials used: composition nesting depth (1, 2, >=3) and composition-type combination counts are the paper's own difficulty dials, with fine-grained combinatorial breakdown in Table 21 (Appendix I.1) showing DRFR falling from 0.845 (1 And) to 0.083 (1 And + 1 Chain + 2 Selection) — useful evidence that stacking constraint TYPES (not just counts) compounds difficulty non-additively.

Constraints per instruction average 4.19 (range ~2-6.3 depending on category), scoring questions per instruction average 4.61 (Table 2) — comparable order of magnitude to c22's 3-5 stacked constraints.

Metric: DRFR (Decomposed Requirements Following Ratio) = fraction of all scoring questions (after dependency-based zeroing) answered 'yes', i.e. partial credit at the sub-question level, NOT the strict all-or-nothing single-instance 0/1 that c22 uses — important floor/ceiling comparison point since c22's stricter scoring will look much harsher than DRFR numbers suggest.

Floors/ceilings observed: best model (GPT-4-1106) = 0.800 DRFR overall (partial-credit metric); on the strict Coherent Test (all-branches-correct within a group, closer in spirit to c22's all-pass philosophy) even GPT-4 falls to 14.9% on multi-layer nested Selection (Sec 5.2.3) — this is a strong empirical prior that all-or-nothing scoring over composed/nested conditional constraints produces very low ceilings, relevant for calibrating c22's baseline difficulty.

Prompt format that swung agreement/accuracy on the evaluation side: one-question-at-a-time with an analysis-then-answer format outperformed presenting all questions simultaneously or forcing bare YES/NO (Appendix E.2 prose) — relevant if c22 ever needs an auxiliary LLM step (e.g. for extraction) even though its core checkers are rule-based.

Rule-based vs LLM-judge split: only ~17% of ComplexBench's scoring questions are rule-verifiable (Table 4); the rest require LLM judgment — this underscores that c22's design choice to draw ALL constraints from a deterministic checker library (rather than ComplexBench's mostly-open-ended constraint bank) is what enables strict, contamination-proof, fully-automatic 0/1 scoring, a property ComplexBench's own constraint pool does not have.

Contamination/data-release status: ComplexBench is a static, manually-annotated, publicly released dataset (1,150 instructions, CC BY 4.0 data / MIT code, GitHub: thu-coai/ComplexBench) built primarily from Chinese real-world instructions then GLM-4-translated to English (Appendix H) — i.e. it is a fixed, potentially contamination-exposed corpus, unlike c22's proposed synthetic/infinite generation approach.

Selection branch position bias is an empirically documented annotation artifact (~70% of two-branch Selections have the correct branch first, Sec 4.1 footnote) that the paper corrects via manual 'Selection Branch Expansion' — a concrete methodological warning for c22's own Selection-constraint generator to avoid an analogous positional/frequency bias in which branch is 'correct'.
quotes: "However, we argue that existing complex instruction-following benchmarks neglect to model the composition of constraints, causing insufficient evaluation of the LLMs' ability to follow complex instructions." (Sec 1, Introduction)

"Selection. The output is required to select different branches according to certain conditions, fulfilling the constraints of the corresponding branch. Formally, Selection contains m branches {B1, B2, ..., Bm}... We denote a selection function as S with a range {1, 2, ..., m}, taking the selection condition cond as input. Finally, the expected output of the instruction is Y_{S(cond)}." (Sec 3.3, Composition)

"To address their limitations, we design a Rule-Augmented LLM-based (RAL) evaluation method to equip LLM evaluators with rules to answer scoring questions in both rule-defined and open-ended areas." (Sec 4.2, Evaluation Protocol)

"Firstly, the widely recognized powerful GPT-4 still fails to complete 20% of complex instructions, highlighting the necessity of complex instruction evaluation. Secondly, as the complexity of composition types within instruction increases, the performance of all LLMs significantly drops, especially on Selection and Chain." (Sec 5.2.2, Main Results)
"We speculate that the main difficulty in Selection lies not only in choosing the correct branch but in executing it without interference from irrelevant branches." (Sec 5.2.2)

"Table 6 shows that GPT-3.5-Turbo-1106 generally performs worse in decomposed instructions, especially as the complexity of composition types within instructions increases. We conjecture that this is due to cumulative errors in multi-round interactions, highlighting that our benchmark is challenging and cannot be simply solved via instruction decomposition." (Sec 5.2.3, Analysis)

"For more complex multi-layer nested Selection instructions, even the state-of-the-art LLM, GPT-4, achieves only 14.9% accuracy in the coherent test. At the same time, smaller-scale LLMs can't perfectly follow any group of instructions." (Sec 5.2.3, The Coherent Test for Selection)

"But the probability of the correct branch appearing at each position in Selection of data constructed by annotators is unequal [footnote: ...in all the selection compositions with two branches, annotators have about a 70% probability of selecting the first branch as the correct one]." (Sec 4.1, Selection Branch Expansion)

corrections: headline_numbers: 'GPT-4-1106 And(1)=0.881 -> Sel.&Chain(>=3)=0.675' used the wrong column — Table 5 shows Sel.&Chain(>=3)=0.626 for GPT-4-1106; 0.675 is actually Sel.&Chain(Avg.). Fixed to 0.626.

models[Qwen1.5-72B-Chat]: claim 'highest open-source model on IFEval/HumanEval/MATH comparison table' was false — Table 7 shows Llama-3-70B-Instruct scores higher than Qwen1.5-72B-Chat on all three (IFEval 78.9 vs 55.8, HumanEval 81.7 vs 71.3, MATH 50.4 vs 42.5). Corrected to state Qwen1.5-72B-Chat is not the highest and cite the actual numbers.

models[InternLM2-20B-Chat/.../ChatGLM3-6B-Chat group]: claim the group's All(Avg.) DRFR range tops out at '0.678 (InternLM2-20B-Chat)' — Table 5 shows the actual max in that group is 0.680 (Qwen1.5-14B-Chat), with InternLM2-20B-Chat at 0.678 a close second. Corrected upper bound and attribution.

failure_modes (last entry): misattributed the word 'powerless' to LLM-based evaluation being weak on rule-defined questions. In the source (Sec 4.2), 'powerless' describes rule-based methods being weak on open-ended questions; LLM-based methods' weakness on rule-defined questions is described as 'a significant deficiency,' not 'powerless.' Corrected to attribute each description to the right method/question-type pairing.
